title: The Age of the Common Man
bullets: "Antebellum" is Latin for "before the war," and it is one of the strangest era labels in history because it names the period entirely by the catastrophe it was sliding toward.

The Constitution protected slavery without using the word, counting an enslaved person as three-fifths of a person to hand slave states extra congressmen and presidential electors their owners controlled.
When Missouri applied to join as a slave state in 1819, the seventy-six-year-old Thomas Jefferson called the fight "a fire bell in the night" that filled him with terror.
The Missouri Compromise of 1820 admitted Missouri slave and Maine free and drew a line across the West at 36 degrees 30 minutes, slavery permitted below it, banned above.
Between 1800 and 1828 almost every state scrapped property requirements for white men, creating near-universal white manhood suffrage, the most democratic electorate in the world by a wide margin.
The same wave of democratization that enfranchised every poor white man stripped free Black men of the vote, with Pennsylvania disenfranchising them by name in its 1838 constitution.
Scholars call this a herrenvolk democracy: radically equal within the dominant racial group precisely because the bottom of the order was occupied by enslaved people defined out of it.
In the 1930s the New Deal's Federal Writers' Project recorded more than two thousand interviews with the last living formerly enslaved people, the largest body of such testimony in existence.

title: King Andrew I
bullets: In 1832 Jackson's enemies circulated a cartoon of him in royal robes trampling the Constitution, captioned "King Andrew the First," drawn by the organized opposition that would become the Whigs.
Jackson had won the most votes in 1824 but lost when the House chose John Quincy Adams, who made Henry Clay secretary of state, the deal Jacksonians branded the "Corrupt Bargain."
Martin Van Buren built the new Democratic Party around Jackson, generally reckoned the oldest continuously operating political party in the world and the direct ancestor of the modern one.
Jackson vetoed the Second Bank of the United States on the ground that it was bad for the common man, transforming the veto from a rare legal check into a routine policy weapon.
After his 1832 landslide Jackson drained the federal deposits into politically friendly "pet banks," firing two treasury secretaries who refused before Roger Taney carried the order out.
Jackson's 1836 Specie Circular forced a run on the over-extended banks, and shortly after he left office the country fell into the Panic of 1837, a depression that lasted into the 1840s.
The Petticoat affair, a society snubbing of the war secretary's wife Peggy Eaton, blew apart Jackson's cabinet, made Calhoun his enemy, and elevated the bachelor Van Buren as his heir.

title: The Nullification Crisis
bullets: In the winter of 1832-33 the United States nearly fought its first civil war thirty years early over a tariff, and every secession argument of 1860-61 was made first, in full, here.
The 1828 "Tariff of Abominations" hurt the South three ways at once, and Southerners came to see it as a Northern majority plundering an agricultural section it could not outvote.
Vice President John C. Calhoun secretly wrote the South Carolina Exposition and Protest, arguing a single state could declare a federal law null and void within its borders.

Calhoun traced nullification to the Kentucky and Virginia Resolutions of 1798, secretly written by Jefferson and Madison against the Alien and Sedition Acts, so the most radical states'-rights weapon was forged by the founders themselves.
South Carolina's 1832 convention passed an Ordinance of Nullification, threatened secession, began arming, and Calhoun resigned the vice presidency to lead the fight from the Senate.

Jackson, himself a slaveholder and a states'-rights man on most questions, called nullification treason, won the Force Bill authorizing military action, and moved federal warships toward Charleston harbor.
Henry Clay's Compromise Tariff of 1833 lowered duties over ten years; South Carolina backed down on the tariff but defiantly nullified the Force Bill to keep the doctrine alive.

title: The Trail of Tears
bullets: The Southeast in 1828 was home to five settled agricultural nations, the Cherokee, Creek, Choctaw, Chickasaw, and Seminole, the political heirs of the mound-building Mississippian world.
White Americans called them the "Five Civilized Tribes," and the Cherokee had a written constitution, a bilingual newspaper, and Sequoyah's syllabary, and it made no difference at all.
The Indian Removal Act of 1830 authorized treaties trading Native land for territory in the West; in practice it was an ultimatum backed by Georgia making Native life impossible.
The Cherokee won Worcester v. Georgia in 1832, ruling Georgia's laws had no force in their territory, and Jackson simply did nothing to enforce the Supreme Court's decision.
The fraudulent Treaty of New Echota, signed by an unauthorized faction of a few hundred, was ratified by a single Senate vote over a petition from sixteen thousand Cherokees.
In 1838 the U.S. Army marched roughly sixteen thousand Cherokee about 800 miles west; the figure most often cited is around 4,000 dead, roughly a quarter of those removed.
The Seminole fought rather than be removed in the Second Seminole War of 1835-1842, the longest, costliest Indian war to that point, ending only when Osceola was seized under a flag of truce.
The land emptied of the five nations became the new cotton heartland; Chapter 4 was, economically, a land-clearing operation for the slave system to grow on.

title: The Market Revolution
bullets: In 1817 a ton of freight took three weeks and cost more than the goods to haul from Buffalo to New York; by 1825 the same ton went by water in about eight days for a tenth of the price.
The Erie Canal, completed in 1825 after eight years of digging, ran 363 miles across New York State and cut the cost of moving freight between the Midwest and the coast by roughly 90 percent.
The Lowell mills recruited young unmarried New England farm women, the "mill girls," housed them under strict supervision, and worked them twelve or more hours a day at the looms.
In 1844 Samuel Morse opened a telegraph line from Washington to Baltimore, decoupling the movement of information from the movement of objects for the first time in human history.
By 1860 the country had roughly 30,000 miles of railroad track, the largest network in the world, running overwhelmingly east-west and wiring the free states into one economic unit.
After the Great Famine began in 1845, over a million desperately poor, overwhelmingly Catholic Irish refugees crowded into Northeastern cities and took the hardest, lowest-paid work.
Anti-Catholic nativism crystallized into the secret-lodge American Party, the "Know-Nothings," briefly one of the largest parties in the country before the slavery question swallowed it whole.

title: King Cotton
bullets: In 1858 Senator James Henry Hammond told the North it dared not make war on the South because if cotton stopped "England would topple headlong" and "Cotton is king."
Cotton was not a self-contained Southern economy but a single integrated Atlantic circuit of enslaved pickers, Northern and British mills, and New York and London financiers.
Eli Whitney's 1793 cotton gin, which cleaned in a moment what had taken a person a day, rescued and supercharged American slavery just as many founders expected it to decline.
The plantation aristocracy who owned twenty or more people was a tiny minority of Southern whites but controlled the South through credit, offices, pulpits, courts, and the press.
The planters offered poor whites a stake that cost them nothing: however poor a white man was, the bottom of the order was reserved by law and race for the enslaved.

Because intensive cotton monoculture exhausts soil within a few decades, the plantation economy did not stay put but moved relentlessly west and southwest into the Black Belt of Alabama and Mississippi.
To the planters a slave system penned within fixed borders was a slave system being slowly strangled, which is the reason no compromise on the territories could ever hold.

title: The Burned-Over District
bullets: Upstate New York was called the "Burned-Over District" because it had been so repeatedly swept by revivalism that, the saying went, there was no unconverted fuel left to burn.
The Second Great Awakening broke with old Calvinist predestination, preaching instead that anyone could choose salvation and a converted person could strive toward moral perfection.

The lawyer-turned-preacher Charles Grandison Finney perfected the machinery of American revivalism in the 1820s and 1830s, the multi-day meeting, the public altar call, and the "anxious bench" of deliberate social pressure.
In 1830 Joseph Smith published the Book of Mormon and organized a new church, claiming a restoration of the original Christianity through continuing revelation to a living prophet.
After Smith was murdered by a mob at Carthage, Illinois, in 1844, Brigham Young led tens of thousands roughly 1,300 miles west to the Great Salt Lake valley, then remote Mexican territory.

Utopian communities like the celibate Shakers and the "complex marriage" Oneida Community tried to build perfect societies from blueprints; the Oneida business outlived its utopia as a tableware brand.

Transcendentalism, gathered in Concord around Emerson and Thoreau, held that conscience outranks any unjust law, and Thoreau's essay "Civil Disobedience" reached, through Mohandas Gandhi a century later, the other side of the planet.

title: The Age of Reform
bullets: The decades from the late 1820s to the Civil War were the most intense period of organized social reform in American history before the twentieth century.
Temperance was the largest reform movement by sheer numbers, and it trained millions, including many women, in how to organize a mass moral movement out of local chapters.
Dorothea Dix spent the 1840s touring jails and almshouses documenting the chained and caged mentally ill, and Horace Mann championed the tax-supported free common school as the "great equalizer."

When Sarah and Angelina Grimke, from a South Carolina slaveholding family, lectured against slavery to mixed audiences, the storm over women speaking in public planted the seed of the women's-rights movement.
Under the doctrine of coverture a married woman had no separate legal existence: she could not own property, keep wages, sign a contract, or sue in her own name.
In 1840 a World Anti-Slavery Convention in London refused to seat its American women, prompting Lucretia Mott and Elizabeth Cady Stanton to resolve to hold a women's convention.
The 1848 Seneca Falls Convention produced Stanton's Declaration of Sentiments, a line-by-line rewrite of the Declaration of Independence whose suffrage demand took seventy-two years to win.
Every other evil the reformers attacked could be improved within the system; slavery could not be improved at all, only abolished, and the South would go to war first.

title: The Peculiar Institution
bullets: The South called slavery "the peculiar institution," a small masterpiece of evasion for the largest system of human bondage in the modern world, which owned about four million people by 1860.
The 1808 ban on the Atlantic slave trade did not shrink slavery; it created a vast internal trade that moved on the order of a million people from the Upper South to the Deep South.

Historians call this forced internal deportation the "Second Middle Passage," and it operated by the systematic destruction of families through sale, the memory recounted most in the testimony itself.
Most enslaved people worked cotton under the gang system, driven dawn to dark under an overseer's whip, or the task system of the rice coast, and the work was extracted by violence.
Against a system designed to deny it, enslaved people built families, an autonomous Christianity, coded spirituals, and preserved African forms from the banjo to call-and-response.
In the founding generation many slaveholders called slavery a "necessary evil," Jefferson's wolf held by the ears, a wrong they claimed to deplore but could see no way out of.
Under the cotton boom and the abolitionist attack that posture inverted into Calhoun's "positive good," and a society that boasts of its central institution cannot be argued out of it.

title: Resistance and Abolition
bullets: In 1831 Garrison launched a tiny abolitionist newspaper and Nat Turner led the deadliest slave revolt in American history, and the white South tied the two together though there was no link.
In 1829 the free Black Bostonian David Walker published an Appeal so incendiary that Southern states made possession a crime and put a price on his head.
Nat Turner and some sixty to seventy followers killed roughly sixty white people in about forty-eight hours; the white reaction slammed shut every remaining crack of freedom with harsher slave codes.
The Underground Railroad helped probably tens of thousands escape; Harriet Tubman went back into slave territory roughly thirteen times and personally guided out about seventy people.
Richard Allen organized the African Methodist Episcopal Church in 1816 as the first fully independent Black denomination, an institutional base for antislavery organizing and mutual aid.
Garrison's immediatism demanded the immediate, uncompensated abolition of slavery as a sin, and Frederick Douglass's 1845 Narrative put the lie to slavery in the words of a man who survived it.
The movement won by provoking self-defeating overreaction, the gag rule and the murder of Elijah Lovejoy convincing Northerners that defending slavery meant suppressing white people's liberties.

title: Manifest Destiny
bullets: In 1845 the editor John O'Sullivan wrote that it was America's "manifest destiny to overspread the continent," naming a belief Americans already acted on without a slogan for it.
Beneath the language of Providence, Manifest Destiny was a doctrine of racial entitlement to a continent, the domestic American twin of the British imperial civilizing mission.

American settlers poured into Mexico's sparsely populated province of Tejas, brought enslaved people with them, and evaded Mexico's 1829 abolition by converting them into nominally lifetime "indentured servants."
In the 1836 Texas Revolution Santa Anna overran the Alamo and its roughly two hundred defenders, including James Bowie and Davy Crockett, turning the defeat into the legend "Remember the Alamo."
At San Jacinto Sam Houston's force overran Santa Anna's army in an assault said to have lasted about eighteen minutes and captured Santa Anna, who signed away the war.
The United States refused to annex the Lone Star Republic for nine years because it meant a vast new slave territory and near-certain war with Mexico.
Expansionists bellowed "Fifty-Four Forty or Fight" over free Oregon and then quietly split it at the 49th parallel, while provoking an actual war over slave-or-contested Texas.

title: The Mexican War
bullets: The U.S.-Mexican War of 1846-1848 is the most consequential American war that most Americans cannot describe, and its consequences are almost entirely about the expansion of slavery.
President Polk wanted California and the Southwest, and after Mexico refused to sell he ordered Zachary Taylor into the disputed strip between the Nueces and the Rio Grande to draw a shot.
Polk told Congress Mexico had "shed American blood upon the American soil," a war the evidence strongly indicates the president engineered, a maneuver later echoed in Stalin's Winter War.
Winfield Scott landed at Veracruz and marched about 260 miles to Mexico City along Cortes's old corridor, taking the capital in six months without losing a battle.
Freshman congressman Abraham Lincoln introduced the Spot Resolutions demanding Polk identify the exact spot where American blood was first shed, and Thoreau went to jail over the war's tax.
In 1846 David Wilmot's Proviso proposed barring slavery from any territory taken from Mexico; it never became law but permanently reframed politics around slavery in the territories.
The 1848 Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo took roughly half of Mexico's territory for fifteen million dollars, and every square mile arrived stamped with the Wilmot Proviso's unanswered question.

title: The Compromise That Wasn't
bullets: The vast new western land sat on the country's politics like an unexploded shell, and the 1850 settlement was a bundle of bargains that bought a few years of false calm.

Gold discovered at a California sawmill in January 1848 detonated the Gold Rush, bringing around 300,000 "forty-niners" from the whole world within a few years and exploding San Francisco from a village overnight.
California applied directly for admission as a free state, which would for the first time give the free states a Senate majority, and Southern leaders began openly discussing secession in 1850.
Clay, Webster, and Calhoun made their final stand in the 1850 Senate debate and were all dead within two years, just as the founding generation's deal-making stopped working.
Stephen A. Douglas passed the Compromise of 1850 in five separate bills: California free, the rest organized with slavery left to settlers, and a drastic new Fugitive Slave Act.
The Fugitive Slave Act denied accused fugitives a jury, paid commissioners more to rule a person enslaved, and conscripted Northerners as slave-catchers, radicalizing people who had not cared.
Enraged by that law, Harriet Beecher Stowe wrote Uncle Tom's Cabin in 1851-52, which sold around 300,000 copies in its first year and made slavery felt as a moral horror by a mass audience.

title: Bleeding Kansas
bullets: For four years after 1850 the country told itself slavery was settled; then in 1854 one senator reopened it on purpose in pursuit of a railroad and the presidency.
To win Southern votes for a Chicago railroad, Stephen A. Douglas wrote the Kansas-Nebraska Act, which explicitly repealed the Missouri Compromise line and declared "popular sovereignty."

Erasing the thirty-four-year-old Missouri line told the North that no limit on slavery's expansion was ever permanent, converting even anti-abolitionist, leave-it-alone moderates into the conviction that the Slave Power could be stopped only by force.
Popular sovereignty turned an abstract question into a contest won by physically getting more of your side's armed people onto the ground, a failure mode echoed in the Partition of Bengal.
The Whig Party split North from South and disintegrated within two years, and into the vacuum came the Republican Party, organized solely against slavery's expansion into the territories.
Pro-slavery Missourian "Border Ruffians" rode in to vote and out again, installing a fraudulent legislature, and Kansas descended into the guerrilla war the newspapers named "Bleeding Kansas."
After the sack of Lawrence, John Brown hacked five pro-slavery settlers to death at Pottawatomie Creek, and Preston Brooks beat Senator Charles Sumner nearly to death on the Senate floor.

title: Dred Scott and the House Divided
bullets: By 1857 the Supreme Court was the only federal branch the slavery question had not yet broken, and the Court's attempt to settle the matter was a total catastrophe.
Dred Scott had been taken by his owner for years onto free soil and sued for his freedom on the long-recognized principle that residence on free soil made a person free.
Chief Justice Roger B. Taney ruled no Black person could be a citizen, in the notorious words that they had "no rights which the white man was bound to respect."
Taney also ruled that Congress could not ban slavery in any territory, declaring the Missouri Compromise unconstitutional all along, only the second time the Court had voided a major federal law.
The decision did the opposite of settling anything, convincing the North that all three branches were captured by the Slave Power, the greatest recruiting event the Republican Party ever had.
Lincoln accepted his 1858 Senate nomination with "A house divided against itself cannot stand," an analysis that the country could not endure permanently half slave and half free.
In October 1859 John Brown raided the federal arsenal at Harpers Ferry; the raid was a fiasco, but the North's mourning of him convinced the South that no Northern party could be trusted.
Everything in the book converges on a single election, the one the South had announced in advance and in plain words it would not accept the result of.
The election was decided before a vote was cast when the Democratic Party split in two and ran Stephen A. Douglas for the North and John C. Breckinridge for the South.
A fourth party, the Constitutional Union Party, ran John Bell of Tennessee on saying as little about slavery as possible, while the Republicans nominated the moderate Abraham Lincoln.
Lincoln won with about 40 percent of the popular vote and a clear Electoral College majority without carrying or even appearing on the ballot in most of the South.
The slave South was now a permanent, outvoted minority, the same dead end the Muslim League reached in late-colonial India, with only structural protection or exit left to it.
The seceding states' own declarations of the causes of secession wrote overwhelmingly and explicitly about slavery, not states' rights in the abstract, a myth constructed after the war.
South Carolina seceded on December 20, 1860, six more Deep South states followed by February 1861, and they formed the Confederate States of America under Jefferson Davis.
The last compromise, the Crittenden Compromise to guarantee slavery below the Missouri line forever, failed because Lincoln's Republicans would not surrender what they had just been elected to do.

title: And the War Came
bullets: Lincoln announced he would send provisions only to the starving Fort Sumter garrison and notified South Carolina in advance, putting the choice to start a war squarely on the Confederacy.
Confederate guns opened on Fort Sumter on April 12, 1861, and the North, divided about coercion, unified almost overnight behind suppressing what it now saw as armed rebellion.
Lincoln's call for 75,000 troops triggered a second secession wave; Virginia brought the Confederacy its greatest industry and Robert E. Lee, who declined Union command and went with his state.
Holding the four slave border states of Delaware, Maryland, Kentucky, and Missouri was decisive, which is why the early Union war aim was preserve the Union, not end slavery.
The Union had roughly 22 million people and the bulk of the country's industry and railroads; the Confederacy had about 9 million and needed a fast win or foreign intervention.
The rifled musket and minie ball gave infantry an accurate range of 300 to 500 yards, but generals kept ordering Napoleonic massed charges, producing industrialized slaughter at Antietam.
Antietam in September 1862 was the bloodiest single day in American history and gave Lincoln the battlefield success he needed to issue the Emancipation Proclamation from strength.
The Proclamation turned every advancing Union army into an army of liberation and made it politically impossible for antislavery Britain or France to intervene for the Confederacy.

title: The Death of Slavery
bullets: The war's second half was the working-out of material logic: the larger industrial side, refusing to break, slowly ground the smaller agrarian one to destruction.
The war turned in one week in July 1863: Lee was stopped at Gettysburg with roughly 50,000 casualties, and the day after, Vicksburg surrendered to Grant, splitting the Confederacy in two.
Roughly 180,000 to 200,000 Black men served in the United States Colored Troops, about a tenth of the Union army, permanently destroying the proslavery argument about Black incapacity.
The Confederate home front collapsed under blockade and inflation, with a Richmond bread riot, while the New York City draft riots became the largest civil insurrection apart from the war itself.
Grant abandoned the search for a decisive battle for grinding attrition, and Sherman marched about 285 miles across Georgia to Savannah destroying the Confederacy's economic capacity.
In the fourth year of a civil war the United States held a free, on-time presidential election the opposition could have won, and Lincoln won re-election carried by soldiers' votes.
Because the Emancipation Proclamation was only a war measure, slavery was killed permanently by the Thirteenth Amendment, abolishing it beyond the reach of any president, court, or state.
Lee surrendered at Appomattox on April 9, 1865; five days later John Wilkes Booth assassinated Lincoln, and the war's death toll, reconstructed at roughly 750,000, earned the era its name.
